skill.name: azure-security-keyvault-secrets-java
skill.description: Reference guide for using the Azure Key Vault Secrets Java SDK to create, retrieve, rotate, back up, and manage secrets.
skill.summary: Use Azure Key Vault Secrets from Java applications with focused SDK examples.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static external-command findings are false positives caused by markdown code fences, not executable shell or Ruby backtick usage. The skill is documentation for Azure Key Vault, but several examples print, hardcode, or write secret material locally, so publication should include a security warning.

medium_findings.title: Secret Values Printed to Console
medium_findings.description: Examples retrieve secret values and print them to standard output. This can expose credentials through logs, terminals, or monitoring systems if copied into real applications.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly call getValue() and print the result. This is a clear credential disclosure risk in documentation users may copy.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded Secret-Like Values in Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill uses realistic password and API key examples. These are documentation samples, but they can encourage unsafe hardcoding patterns.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values appear in example code, not active runtime code. The risk is still meaningful because the examples handle secrets and may be copied.

medium_findings.title: Secret Backup Written to Local File
medium_findings.description: The backup example writes Key Vault secret backup bytes to a local file and reads them back without mentioning storage protection, access control, or cleanup.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are explicit and handle backup material for secrets. The example lacks safeguards, although it is documentation rather than executable skill code.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as External Commands
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick findings point to fenced markdown examples. I found no evidence that the skill itself executes shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is markdown fencing around XML, Java, and bash examples. There is no script file or instruction that runs these blocks automatically.

low_findings.title: Expected Azure Vault URLs
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL detections are Azure Key Vault endpoint examples used to configure the SDK. They do not show exfiltration to an unrelated service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network endpoints are placeholders or normal Azure Key Vault hostnames. This aligns with the skill purpose and does not indicate malicious networking.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Detection Is an Ellipsis False Positive
low_findings.description: The path traversal finding points to an escaped JSON string containing placeholder ellipses, not filesystem traversal or path construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a JSON configuration example with placeholder text. It does not reference file paths, relative path segments, or user-controlled filesystem access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret Values Logged
dangerous_patterns.description: Documentation examples log retrieved secret values. Users should redact values and log only metadata such as secret names or versions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly print getValue() results. This is a risky pattern for secret-management guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Secret Backup File
dangerous_patterns.description: The backup example writes sensitive backup bytes to local storage. Real use should protect, encrypt, and clean up backup files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file write and read are direct and handle secret backup material. Missing operational safeguards create a practical documentation risk.

content.user_title: Manage Azure Key Vault Secrets in Java
content.value_statement: Java teams need reliable patterns for storing and rotating sensitive configuration in Azure. This skill provides Azure Key Vault Secrets SDK examples that Claude, Codex, and Claude Code can adapt.
actual_capabilities: Shows Maven dependency setup for the Azure Key Vault Secrets Java SDK.
Demonstrates synchronous and asynchronous SecretClient creation with Azure Identity.
Covers creating, retrieving, updating, deleting, recovering, and purging secrets.
Includes examples for listing secrets, versions, and secret properties.
Provides patterns for loading multiple secrets and rotating secret versions.
Explains backup, restore, environment variable, and common secret type examples.
limitations: Does not provide a complete runnable Java project or tests.
Uses placeholder and sample secret values that should not be copied into production.
Does not cover Azure IAM setup, network controls, or Key Vault provisioning.
Some examples print secret values and need redaction before production use.

use_cases.title: Add Key Vault to a Java Service
use_cases.description: Use the client creation and get secret examples to connect an application to Azure Key Vault.

use_cases.title: Review Secret Rotation Logic
use_cases.description: Use the rotation example to draft version-based replacement flows for credentials.

use_cases.title: Document Secret Management Patterns
use_cases.description: Use the property, listing, and best practice sections to write team guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to show how to create an Azure Key Vault Secrets client in Java with DefaultAzureCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Retrieve a Secret Safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft Java code that retrieves a named secret without printing the secret value.

prompt_templates.title: Rotate a Secret
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a Java secret rotation flow that creates a new version and disables the old version.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Production Hardening
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review a Java Key Vault integration for logging, backup handling, expiration, and least privilege gaps.

output_examples.input: Help me connect a Java service to Azure Key Vault.
output_examples.output: A short implementation plan with dependency setup, SecretClient creation, vault URL configuration, and credential prerequisites.

output_examples.input: Show a safer version of the get secret example.
output_examples.output: Guidance that retrieves the secret for application use while logging only the secret name, version, and success status.

output_examples.input: Explain how to rotate a database password.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step rotation outline covering current version lookup, new version creation, old version disablement, and rollback checks.

best_practices: Redact secret values in logs, errors, examples, and generated documentation.
Use DefaultAzureCredential with least-privilege Key Vault roles in each environment.
Set expiration, tags, and version management rules for secrets that require rotation.
anti_patterns: Copying sample passwords or API keys into real source code.
Printing retrieved secret values during debugging or error handling.
Saving Key Vault backup blobs to unmanaged local files.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands?
faq.answer: No. It is a markdown reference with Java SDK examples, not an automation script.

faq.question: Which Azure SDK package does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers the Azure Key Vault Secrets Java SDK package com.azure:azure-security-keyvault-secrets.

faq.question: Can it provision an Azure Key Vault?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on Java client usage after a vault and permissions already exist.

faq.question: Can the examples be used in production directly?
faq.answer: They need review first. Replace sample values, redact logs, and add operational safeguards.

faq.question: Does it include asynchronous usage?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples using SecretAsyncClient for set, get, and list operations.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation, but some examples print or store sensitive secret material.
